title: Draught-proofing a sash window
subtitle: Fitting pile seals and brush strips to reduce air leakage around the sashes
excerpt: An unweatherstripped sash window can leak as much air as leaving a 150 mm hole in the wall. Fitting pile seals into the meeting rail, brush strips to the top and bottom sashes, and brush or wiper seals to the pulley stiles cuts infiltration by 80-90% while keeping the window fully operational.
sourceNotes: Historic England (formerly English Heritage) guidance on upgrading sash windows while retaining their character. Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing page for typical savings. AECB Warm House Retrofit Guide for air-leakage reduction. Building Regulations Part L1B for airtightness targets in existing dwellings.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sealing the gaps around operable building elements (windows, doors, loft hatches) to prevent uncontrolled air infiltration.

content.text: A sash window leaks air at four points: the meeting rail, the bottom of the lower sash, the top of the upper sash, and the two pulley stiles (the channels the sashes run in). Fitting
draught seals

to all four closes 80-90% of the air leakage without fixing the window shut. Materials cost around a modest cost; a terrace with eight sash windows on the ground and first floor typically saves 1,200-2,000 kWh/year on a gas-heated house, worth a modest cost at 2025/26 cap rates of 6p/kWh gas.
The four seal positions

Meeting rail: the horizontal joint where the upper and lower sash overlap when the window is closed. This is usually the largest leakage point. Fit pile (mohair strip) into a routed groove in the rail of each sash, or use self-adhesive pile strip.
Bottom of lower sash: fits against the window board. A brush strip or wiper seal screwed to the bottom rail of the lower sash sweeps against the sill when the window is closed.
Top of upper sash: fits against the head of the outer frame. A brush strip screwed to the top rail of the upper sash or into the frame head closes this gap.

Pulley stiles: the side channels the sashes slide in. Pile strip fitted into the side of each sash (face-fixed or routed) runs against the parting bead as the sash moves. This is the hardest seal to fit and the one most often left undone.
Materials
Mohair pile strip (also called brush strip), 5 x 5 mm, in a self-adhesive or screw-fixed carrier: measure the perimeter of both sashes and order 20% extra
Wiper or blade seal for the bottom rail: flexible rubber or EPDM strip, 25 mm wide, length of the lower sash width
Small wood screws (No.6, 16 mm) for fixing carriers to sash rails
Installation
Clean all the sash surfaces with a cloth and methylated spirits. Self-adhesive pile strip does not bond to dirty or flaking paint.

Raise the lower sash and lower the upper sash. You now have access to the meeting rails of both. Apply pile strip to the top face of the lower sash meeting rail (facing into the room) and the bottom face of the upper sash meeting rail (facing the outside). Each pile faces the other and they compress together when the window is shut.

Lower the upper sash fully. Apply a brush strip to the underside of the top rail of the upper sash or to the head of the frame immediately above it. Check that the strip doesn't prevent the sash from closing.
Raise the lower sash fully. Apply a wiper seal to the underside of the bottom rail. Reclose the sash and check the seal sweeps lightly over the window board without binding.
Fit the pulley-stile pile strips on the edges of each sash. The pile must face outward against the parting bead. Check that both sashes slide smoothly without the pile folding over.
Listed buildings

The sealing methods described here involve no structural alteration and are removable. They do not require listed building consent. External works (new windows, secondary glazing within a listed window aperture) are a different matter and need a listed building application.
What done well looks like vs done badly

Done well: both sashes slide with light resistance at the stiles, the meeting rail seals when latched, and a lit match at each gap shows no flicker. Done badly: pile strip folds and prevents closing, adhesive strips peel within a season, and stile channels stay unsealed.

attrs.symptom: Sash binds and sticks after fitting pile
attrs.cause: Pile strip too thick for the gap at the pulley stile, or pile folded over in the groove
attrs.fix: Replace with a thinner pile (3 x 3 mm instead of 5 x 5 mm) or reroute the strip so the pile faces cleanly against the parting bead

attrs.symptom: Self-adhesive strip falls off within weeks
attrs.cause: Adhesive applied to unprepared painted surface
attrs.fix: Clean the surface with isopropyl alcohol, let dry fully, then apply new strip using screw-fixed carrier rather than adhesive

attrs.symptom: Draught persists at the pulley stile after sealing
attrs.cause: Pulley box itself is open to the outside air (common in older sashes)
attrs.fix: Pack the pulley box with mineral wool (or a plug of foam) around the cords, leaving the pulleys free to move
